title: Water and Its Biological Properties
subject: AP Biology
objectives: Explain how water's polarity and hydrogen bonding give rise to its unique properties
Describe how cohesion, adhesion, and high specific heat support life
Analyze why the density anomaly of ice is critical for aquatic ecosystems

sections.heading: The Molecular Shape That Makes Life Possible
sections.content:
Water looks simple — just two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen — but its molecular geometry is one of the most consequential facts in all of biology. The oxygen atom is far more electronegative than hydrogen, meaning it pulls the shared electrons closer to itself. This creates a polar molecule: the oxygen end carries a partial negative charge (δ−) and each hydrogen end carries a partial positive charge (δ+).

Think of it like a tiny magnet. The negative end of one water molecule attracts the positive end of another. These attractions are called hydrogen bonds. Individually, a single hydrogen bond is weak — roughly twenty times weaker than the covalent bond holding hydrogen to oxygen within the molecule. But water molecules form enormous networks of hydrogen bonds simultaneously, and that collective strength gives water almost every property that life depends on.

You live on a planet where the temperature, pressure, and chemistry happen to keep water in all three phases — solid, liquid, and gas — at Earth's surface. That is not common in the universe. And it is water's polarity that makes this range of behavior possible.

sections.heading: Cohesion, Adhesion, and Surface Tension
sections.content:
Cohesion is the attraction of water molecules to each other through hydrogen bonds. Adhesion is the attraction of water molecules to other polar surfaces — like the walls of a glass tube or the cellulose in plant cell walls.

Together, cohesion and adhesion power capillary action: water can climb upward through narrow tubes against gravity. In a plant, this is how water travels from the roots through the xylem to the leaves, sometimes rising over 100 meters in the tallest trees. The evaporation of water from leaf surfaces (transpiration) pulls on a continuous chain of water molecules held together by cohesion. Adhesion keeps the column clinging to the xylem walls.

Surface tension — the tendency of water's surface to resist disruption — is also a result of cohesion. Water molecules at the surface have no neighbors above them, so they hydrogen-bond more tightly to the molecules beside and below them. This creates a surprisingly sturdy film. That is why certain insects can walk on water, and why a carefully placed paperclip can float despite being denser than water.

sections.heading: High Specific Heat in Action
sections.content:
Water's specific heat — the amount of energy needed to raise 1 gram of water by 1°C — is 4.18 J/(g·°C), which is exceptionally high compared to most substances.

Imagine two pots on a stove: one with a cup of water, one with a cup of vegetable oil. The oil heats up much faster, because its specific heat is roughly half that of water. Water resists temperature change because incoming heat energy is spent breaking and rearranging hydrogen bonds before the molecules actually speed up (which is what temperature measures).

Biological consequence: your body is about 60% water. That enormous thermal buffer prevents your internal temperature from spiking every time you exercise or step into sunlight. On a planetary scale, oceans absorb huge amounts of solar energy during the day and release it slowly at night, moderating coastal climates. Without water's high specific heat, temperature swings on Earth would be extreme enough to make most life impossible.
Water's polarity leads to hydrogen bonding, which produces cohesion, adhesion, high specific heat, and solvent ability — the properties that make water uniquely suited to support life.

sections.heading: Water as the Universal Solvent
sections.content:
When you stir table salt (NaCl) into water, the positive ends of water molecules surround the negative chloride ions, and the negative ends surround the positive sodium ions. This process — hydration — pulls the crystal apart ion by ion. Any substance that dissolves in water is called hydrophilic ("water-loving"), and substances that do not dissolve are hydrophobic ("water-fearing").

This matters enormously for biology. Nearly every chemical reaction inside your cells takes place in an aqueous (water-based) solution. Enzymes, ions, sugars, amino acids — they all need to be dissolved in water to interact. Cell membranes themselves are organized by the hydrophobic effect: phospholipids arrange into bilayers because their nonpolar tails are pushed away from water while their polar heads face toward it.

sections.heading: The Ice Density Anomaly
sections.content:
Most substances are denser as solids than as liquids — their molecules pack more tightly when they freeze. Water does the opposite. As water cools below 4°C, the hydrogen bonds lock into a crystalline lattice with more space between molecules than liquid water has. Ice is therefore about 9% less dense than liquid water, which is why it floats.

This single fact has staggering consequences for life. When a lake freezes in winter, ice forms at the surface first and acts as an insulating blanket. The liquid water below stays near 4°C — cold, but survivable for fish, amphibians, and countless microorganisms. If ice sank, lakes would freeze from the bottom up, killing most aquatic life each winter and potentially never fully thawing during summer.

Earth's climate would also be radically different. Floating sea ice reflects sunlight (the albedo effect), helping regulate global temperatures. The density anomaly of ice is not a quirky footnote — it is one of the structural pillars holding Earth's biosphere together.

aiContext: AP Biology. Connect molecular mechanisms to organism and ecosystem outcomes. Stress experimental design and data analysis. Common confusion: transcription vs translation, and when to apply Hardy-Weinberg conditions.
